title: Garden Harvest Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Your Expected Garden Harvest Yield
description: Estimate vegetable garden harvest yields by crop type. Get annual kg/m² estimates instantly, completely free.
intro: The Garden Harvest Calculator estimates how many kilograms of vegetables, fruits, or herbs a home garden plot can realistically produce in a single growing season. It uses per-crop yield benchmarks (kg/m²) established by university extension programs and the USDA, multiplied by the area you plant. Whether you're planning a raised bed, a backyard vegetable patch, or a community garden plot, knowing your expected yield helps you avoid overplanting, calculate food preservation needs, and size your storage. The core formula is: **Expected Yield (kg) = Yield Rate (kg/m²) × Planted Area (m²)**, where the yield rate varies by crop, climate, soil quality, and growing method.
keyTakeaway: **Expected Yield (kg) = Yield Rate (kg/m²) × Area (m²)** — e.g., tomatoes at 3 kg/m² over 10 m² = 30 kg/season; lettuce at 2 kg/m² over 5 m² = 10 kg/season.
useCases: Calculating how many pounds of tomatoes a 4×8 ft raised bed (≈3 m²) will produce to decide how many jars of sauce to prepare for canning season.
Planning a community garden plot allocation: determining if 20 m² of mixed vegetables can supply a family of four with enough produce for 6 months.
Estimating zucchini overproduction risk — at 4 kg/m², even a 3 m² plot yields ~12 kg, helping gardeners decide to plant only 1–2 m².
Sizing a root cellar or chest freezer before harvest: a 15 m² potato bed at 3.5 kg/m² means storing ~52 kg of potatoes.
Comparing growing methods — determining whether switching from in-ground beds to raised beds (which can boost yield by 20–30%) justifies the setup cost for a specific crop.
Budgeting seed and fertilizer purchases proportionally to planned harvest volume for market gardeners selling at local farmers' markets.
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The calculator applies a single core formula per crop:

```
Expected Yield (kg) = Yield Rate (kg/m²) × Planted Area (m²)

Example — Tomatoes:
  Yield Rate = 3.0 kg/m²
  Area       = 10 m²
  Result     = 3.0 × 10 = 30 kg/season

Example — Green Beans:
  Yield Rate = 1.2 kg/m²
  Area       = 6 m²
  Result     = 1.2 × 6 = 7.2 kg/season
```

Yield rates represent **average home-garden productivity** under adequate watering, standard fertilization (N-P-K balanced), and full sun (≥6 h/day). Controlled environments (greenhouses, grow lights) can increase rates by 30–60%; shaded or compacted-soil beds may reduce them by 20–40%.

---

## Reference Table — Common Crop Yield Rates

Benchmarks sourced from USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service data and university cooperative extension guidelines (UC Davis, Penn State Extension):

| Crop | Yield Rate (kg/m²) | Season | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tomato (indeterminate) | 2.5 – 4.0 | Summer | Stakes/cages required; avg ~3.0 |
| Tomato (determinate/bush) | 1.5 – 2.5 | Summer | Shorter harvest window |
| Zucchini / Summer Squash | 3.5 – 5.0 | Summer | Very high output; 1–2 plants/m² |
| Bell Pepper | 1.2 – 2.0 | Summer | Avg ~1.5 kg/m² in open beds |
| Cucumber | 2.0 – 3.5 | Summer | Trellised plants yield upper range |
| Green Beans (bush) | 0.8 – 1.5 | Summer | ~1.2 avg; succession sowing recommended |
| Lettuce (loose-leaf) | 1.5 – 2.5 | Spring/Fall | Multiple cuts; 3–4 harvests/season |
| Spinach | 1.0 – 1.8 | Spring/Fall | Cool-season; bolts in heat |
| Kale | 1.5 – 2.2 | Spring/Fall | Cut-and-come-again method |
| Carrot | 2.0 – 3.0 | Spring/Fall | Depends heavily on soil depth |
| Potato | 2.5 – 4.5 | Summer | ~3.5 avg; needs hilling |
| Garlic | 0.8 – 1.2 | Fall plant / Summer harvest | ~1.0 avg |
| Onion | 1.5 – 2.5 | Spring/Summer | Sets vs. seed affects yield |
| Strawberry | 0.5 – 1.0 | Spring/Summer | First year lower; peaks year 2–3 |
| Pumpkin | 4.0 – 8.0 | Summer | Sprawling; needs 3–5 m²/plant |

> **Note:** 1 kg ≈ 2.205 lbs. To convert: multiply kg result by 2.205 for pounds.

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1 — Backyard Salad Garden (8 m²)
A gardener plants 3 m² of lettuce and 2 m² of spinach in spring, plus 3 m² of kale for fall:
- Lettuce: 2.0 kg/m² × 3 m² = **6 kg**
- Spinach: 1.4 kg/m² × 2 m² = **2.8 kg**
- Kale: 1.8 kg/m² × 3 m² = **5.4 kg**
- **Total: ~14.2 kg of salad greens** across two mini-seasons from 8 m².

### Case 2 — Tomato & Pepper Raised Bed (6 m²)
A 4×6 ft raised bed (≈2.2 m²) of indeterminate tomatoes + a 4×4 ft bed (≈1.5 m²) of bell peppers, plus a 2 m² cucumber trellis:
- Tomatoes: 3.0 × 2.2 = **6.6 kg**
- Peppers: 1.5 × 1.5 = **2.25 kg**
- Cucumbers: 2.8 × 2.0 = **5.6 kg**
- **Total: ~14.5 kg** — enough for ~50 servings of fresh produce.

### Case 3 — Potato Storage Bed (20 m²)
A household dedicates a 4×5 m plot exclusively to Yukon Gold potatoes:
- 3.5 kg/m² × 20 m² = **70 kg of potatoes**
- At 150 g per medium potato: roughly **466 potatoes**, or ~1.3 kg/person/week for a family of 4 over 13 weeks.

---

## Common Errors

1. **Using commercial farm yields instead of garden yields.** USDA commercial tomato yields average 8–10 kg/m² in optimized field conditions; home gardens with standard care produce 2.5–4.0 kg/m². Applying farm rates will grossly overestimate your harvest.

2. **Ignoring plant spacing requirements.** Zucchini needs 0.5–1 m² per plant at minimum. Cramming 5 plants into 2 m² doesn't multiply yield — it reduces air circulation, increases disease risk, and cuts per-plant output by up to 50%.

3. **Counting the full calendar area, not the planted area.** A 10 m² bed is only productive when plants occupy it. Walking paths, uncovered soil between transplants, and succession-planting gaps mean the "effective yield area" is often 70–85% of the bed's total footprint.

4. **Forgetting succession planting offsets.** If you plant two batches of lettuce 3 weeks apart, the total yield per m² can approach 4.0 kg over a season — double the single-sowing rate. The calculator uses single-crop defaults; manual adjustments are needed for succession plans.

5. **Applying flat rates to first-year perennials.** Strawberries, asparagus, and artichokes yield far below their peak in year one. Asparagus yields near zero for the first 2 years; strawberries may produce only 30–50% of mature-plant rates in year one.

6. **Not adjusting for climate zone.** USDA Plant Hardiness Zones affect season length. A zone 5 gardener gets ~120 frost-free days vs. ~250 in zone 9, directly cutting how many succession crops — or how large — tomato plants can grow.

---

faq.q: What is the average tomato yield per square meter in a home garden?
faq.a: Home garden tomato yields typically range from 2.5 to 4.0 kg/m² per season for indeterminate (vining) varieties like Cherokee Purple or Brandywine. Bush/determinate types average 1.5–2.5 kg/m². These figures assume full sun (6+ hours/day), consistent watering, and basic fertilization. USDA commercial benchmarks are much higher (8–10 kg/m²) and should not be used for backyard estimates.

faq.q: How do raised beds compare to in-ground gardens for yield?
faq.a: University extension research (Penn State, Cornell) consistently shows raised beds yield 20–30% more per square meter than in-ground plots. The main reasons: better drainage, looser soil improving root penetration, faster soil warming in spring, and easier weed/pest management. For example, a tomato bed yielding 3.0 kg/m² in-ground might produce 3.6–3.9 kg/m² in a raised bed with quality soil mix.

faq.q: Does the calculator account for multiple harvests per season (succession planting)?
faq.a: The default yield rates reflect a single planting cycle. Fast-growing crops like lettuce, radishes, and spinach can be planted 2–4 times per season in the same space. If you succession-plant lettuce (≈45-day cycle) three times in a spring season, your effective yield can reach 4–6 kg/m² instead of the default 2 kg/m². Multiply the single-cycle rate by the number of planned successions for a more accurate total.

faq.q: How much area do I need to feed a family of four in vegetables for a summer?
faq.a: The USDA recommends approximately 200–400 sq ft (18–37 m²) of mixed vegetables per person for a significant portion of summer produce. For a family of four, a productive 50–75 m² garden with mixed crops (tomatoes, beans, zucchini, greens) can realistically provide 200–350 kg of produce per season — a meaningful supplement but not full dietary replacement for most families.

faq.q: What crops give the highest yield per square meter for small spaces?
faq.a: For maximum kg/m² in limited space, prioritize: zucchini (3.5–5.0 kg/m²), indeterminate tomatoes on trellises (up to 4.0 kg/m²), and cucumbers on vertical supports (up to 3.5 kg/m²). Vertical growing for vining crops effectively doubles usable growing area. Avoid pumpkins, melons, and corn in small plots — they have low yield-per-footprint ratios relative to the space they consume.

faq.q: How does USDA Plant Hardiness Zone affect expected yield?
faq.a: Your USDA zone determines frost-free days, which cap the growing season length and the size/maturity of warm-season crops. Zone 5 (e.g., Chicago) has ~120–150 frost-free days; Zone 9 (e.g., Phoenix) has 240–270. In shorter-season zones, indeterminate tomatoes may only reach 50–70% of their potential yield before frost, effectively dropping average yields from 3.0 to 1.5–2.0 kg/m². Check your zone at the USDA Plant Hardiness Zone Map.

faq.q: Are the yield rates in kilograms or pounds — and how do I convert?
faq.a: This calculator outputs results in kilograms (kg), the standard metric unit. To convert to pounds, multiply by 2.205. For example, a 30 kg tomato yield equals approximately 66.2 lbs. To convert m² area inputs from square feet: 1 m² = 10.764 sq ft, so a standard 4×8 ft raised bed (32 sq ft) ≈ 2.97 m², which you would round to 3 m² when entering the value.

faq.q: Why is my actual harvest lower than the calculator's estimate?
faq.a: The most common causes are: (1) insufficient sunlight — yields drop 30–50% with less than 6 hours of direct sun; (2) inconsistent watering — tomatoes need ~2.5 cm of water/week and yield far less under drought stress; (3) soil nutrient deficiency — a basic soil test (available through many county extension offices for $15–30) can reveal pH or NPK imbalances; and (4) pest/disease loss, which the USDA estimates reduces home garden harvests by 10–25% on average nationwide.

faq.q: Can I use this calculator for herbs like basil or cilantro?
faq.a: Yes, though herbs are typically measured in grams rather than kilograms because their fresh weight yield per m² is much lower than vegetables. Basil yields roughly 0.3–0.6 kg/m² per season of fresh leaves; cilantro yields 0.2–0.5 kg/m². Enter these values manually as custom yield rates if your calculator allows it, or convert: 0.5 kg/m² over 1 m² = 500 g of fresh basil, which equals about 50+ servings at typical recipe quantities.

sources.name: USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service — Vegetables Summary
Penn State Extension — Home Vegetable Gardening

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
